record.source: Amerikastudien/American Studies: A Quarterly
record.title: 'To Make Sense of a Random Act of Violence': Tyche, Automaton, and Trauma in Charles Brockden Brown's Edgar Huntly, or, Memoirs of a Sleep-Walker
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Edgar Huntly (1799) ; novel ; trauma ; chaos ; rationality
record.abstract: Taking its cue from Charles Brockden Brown's use of a number of ' proto-chaotic' terms in his novel Edgar Huntly: Memoirs of a Sleep-Walker— such as contingency, chance, catastrophe, probability, fluctuation, turbulence, and swerve—, this essay traces some aspects of the novel's seemingly inconsistent' structure, reading it as the effect of a carefully worked-out ' chaotic poetics.' In a second step, it connects the chaotic references with the notion of trauma, the logic and temporality of which are the matrices in which the novel might be read. The connection between chaos and trauma happens through the Aristotelian term ' tyche' and the Lucretian term ' clinamen.' Both of these terms, which are used in the novel at crucial moments, are also used by Lacan to denote the ' topology of trauma.' Ultimately, the essay argues, the novel highlights a danger expressed in the ambiguous statement that ' rationality is irrational'; an ambiguity also pervading Goya's statement that 'el sueno de la razon produce monstruos.'

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Melville's Long Ghost and Smollett's Count Fathom
record.subjects: American literature ; 1800-1899 ; Melville, Herman (1819-1891) ; Omoo (1847) ; novel ; Long Ghost, Dr. (character) ; Fathom, Ferdinand Count (character) ; Smollett, Tobias (1721-1771) ; Ferdinand Count Fathom (1753) ; Scottish literature ; 1700-1799
record.abstract: According to his own account, Melville read Smollett's novels while he was wandering in the South Seas. His confession in +Omoo=-"you hero of rogues, Count Fathom! -what a debt we owe you!"-suggests not only the pleasure he received from reading one of his favorite authors but also his debt in characterizing that congenial rogue, Long Ghost. The two characters are strikingly similar in their backgrounds, their pretensions to almost universal knowledge, their robust sexual appetites, and their great abilities as entertainers. How much Long Ghost resembles Melville's actual companion while on Tahiti, John Troy, is now difficult to say, but it is highly likely that Melville's "doctor" owes even more to that other one-time pretender in medicine (and all-round fraud), Count Fathom.

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Ormond: Seduction in a New Key
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Ormond (1799) ; novel ; seduction
record.abstract: A consideration of Brown's relationship to the literary traditions he was working with in using the destruction motif-i.e., the sentimental, gothic, and purposive-discloses a richness of development in the concluding action that is unrivaled in the fiction preceding +Ormond=. Brown accomplishes this chiefly by giving his principals some complexity of character. Ormond, who initially regards Constantia as 'an exquisite wretch,' soon becomes impressed with her moral strength; however, when frustrated in love, he must exercise the intelligence she admires in the service of an unseemly seduction. Constantia, meanwhile, is so keenly attracted to the man that she obstinately refuses to see his motive even when he openly declares it. Ironically, Ormond achieves rational mastery in confuting Constantia's conventional sex morality, while she gains a physical mastery which leaves her with strange feelings of remorse-a situation quite unlike that of the stereotyped 'pursued maiden.'

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Further Verification of the Authorship of The Power of Sympathy
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, William Hill (1765-1793) ; The Power of Sympathy (1789) ; novel ; attribution of authorship
record.abstract: Arthur W. Brayley's 1894 attribution of +The Power of Sympathy= to William Hill Brown on the basis of the verification by Mrs. Rebecca V. Thompson. Brown's niece, Mrs. Thompson's own signed statement in 1900, a presentation copy of the novel, and a comment in a 1789 letter now in the manuscript collection of the Massachusetts Historical Society from Mrs. Margaret Mascarene Hutchinson of Halifax, Nova Scotia, should establish with certainty the authorship of the first American novel.

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Arthur Mervyn, American
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Arthur Mervyn (1799-1800) ; novel ; morality ; success ; Mervyn, Arthur (character) ; American dream
record.abstract: The importance of Charles Brockden Brown's +Arthur Mervyn= (1799-1800), both as a coherent novel and as a precursor to later and better fiction, lies not so much in its gothicism as in its depiction of a new kind of hero: the successful and moral American. This homiletic +Bildungsroman= traces its country-bred protagonist's success in overcoming urban threats to his innocence. Despite frequent expressions of ignorance and a desire to learn, Mervyn displays a stubborn faith in his rigid moral code which contradicts that in his new experiences. Aware, however, that his destiny will be realized in the city, Mervyn assimilates enough of an urban ethos to insinuate himself into the confidences of older gentlemen who can help him; armed only with what he calls "good intentions," he associates with seducers, forgers, prostitutes, and murderers. Brown creates, for the first time in our literature, an ambiguity-ridden hero endowed with distinctive traits of a national character, one whose role is dual: to seek success in an urban, commercial society and to seek it on his own terms-by using, not losing, his ignorance, innocence, and gullibility, and by making misfortunes opportunities not so much for learning as for teaching.

record.subjects: American literature ; 1800-1899 ; Melville, Herman (1819-1891) ; Billy Budd ; novel ; human nature ; Hobbes, Thomas (1588-1679) ; Rousseau, Jean-Jacques (1712-1778) ; French literature ; 1700-1799 ; prose

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Studied Ambiguities: Arthur Mervyn and the Problem of the Unreliable Narrator
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Arthur Mervyn (1799-1800) ; novel ; unreliable narrator
record.abstract: Charles Brockden Brown's +Arthur Mervyn= is a novel in which social milieu is a real environment conditioning its characters in an immediate way. +Arthur Mervyn= provides not only a pre-Freudian portrait of a character whose experiences reveal Oedipal feelings, but of a young man who is moved by the ambitions and anxieties fostered by the American myth of success. The full possibilities of the narrative's social and psychological realism seem to have occurred to Brown in the midst of composition. From a simple demonstration of the ways in which a young man from the country resists the temptations of the city, Brown shifted to an attempt to convey the ironic interplay between Arthur's conscious and unconscious motivation and the sense of bewilderment that results from the ambiguity of appearances. Brown's new vision led him to experiment with the techniques of multiple perspective and the device of the unreliable narrator. Arthur is not a young man haunted by gothic terror, but a young American on the make in a competitive, moralistic business culture.

record.source: American Literature: A Journal of Literary History, Criticism, and Bibliography
record.title: Thomas Atwood Digges and Adventures of Alonso: Evidence from Robert Southey
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Digges, Thomas Attwood (1741-1821) ; Adventures of Alonso (1775) ; novel ; letters ; Southey, Robert (1774-1843) ; attribution of authorship
record.abstract: Since 1941, circumstantial and internal evidence has constituted the only basis for attributing the writing of +Adventures of Alonso: Containing Some Striking Anecdotes of the Present Prime Minister of Portugal= (London, 1775) to Thomas Atwood Digges of Warburton, Maryland, and consequently for calling that book the first American novel. Recent discovery of explicit references to Digges and his authorship in unpublished portions of the autobiographical letters of Robert Southey confirms the attribution and supports the novel's claim to primacy.

record.source: Nineteenth-Century Fiction
record.title: Brockden Brown's Other Novels: Clara Howard and Jane Talbot
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Clara Howard (1801) ; Jane Talbot (1801) ; novel ; narrative structure ; point of view ; characterization
record.abstract: Brown's themes of restraint and benevolence and his moral tone dominate all his novels. Gothic egos and artists or artist surrogates are condemned and converted to social ends, whatever the cost to these individuals or Brown's technique. That Brown's self-defeating view of art is consistent is shown by the motif of the evil benefactor, a deadly man in the early novels and, in later novels, an uncompromising woman clutching the hero to the heart of society. +Clara Howard= and +Jane Talbot= are logical ends of Brown's quest for form. They are tightly structured and the letter form creates good effects of suspense, irony, and shifting points of view. +Jane Talbot= offers Brown's most believable villain, Frank, and Jane, his most interesting woman character. Caught between his ethic vision and his moral conservatism, Brown ended his writing career not knowing that the form he had found in his last two novels was his most effective compromise.
record.longDBName: MLA International Bibliography with Full Text

record.source: Nineteenth-Century Fiction
record.title: The Case against Carwin
record.subjects: American literature ; 1700-1799 ; Brown, Charles Brockden (1771-1810) ; Wieland (1798) ; Memoirs of Carwin the Biloquist (1815) ; novel ; Carwin, Francis (character)
record.abstract: Charles Brockden Brown's +Wieland= and +Memoirs of Carwin= are linked by an oedipal theme, a theme found also in Brown's life and throughout his creative work. Carwin is the rebellious son, Wieland the surrogate father, and Clara the mother figure. Carwin's life is a long tale of trouble-making, deception, pleas of innocence, and feelings of guilt. He reacts to his natural father's oppression by going to Europe to study under the satanic Ludloe; in +Wieland= he returns to America and destroys the surrogate father. The tragedy of +Wieland=, therefore, is merely the final stage of Carwin's desire to revenge himself through his "supernatural" power and mask of biloquism, against those who slip into the antagonistic role of oppressor. This interpretation explains why Brown felt compelled to begin +Carwin= immediately upon finishing +Wieland=: he subconsciously felt that he had to dramatize the origin and early career of his major character, Carwin. The key oedipal scene is in +Carwin= when Carwin enters his father's bedroom during a storm. After this, the novel loses momentum, because Brown had already treated the climax of the motif in +Wieland=.
